steps.title: Identify what would refute the feared outcome
steps.content:
The concern was that virtual tours would discourage in-person visits by making people think they've already seen the art.

For the administrators' claim (that concerns are unjustified) to be supported, survey evidence must show that virtual tours did not discourage visits—or actively encouraged them.

steps.title: Match the evidence to the claim
steps.content:
Choice B states that many surveyed visitors indicated the virtual tours convinced them to plan an in-person visit. This directly proves the feared outcome did not occur; instead, virtual tours had the opposite effect and actually motivated in-person visits.

The other choices discuss visitor origins (A), reminiscing after visits (C), or pre-visit awareness gaps (D), none of which directly show that virtual tours failed to prevent—or succeeded in promoting—in-person visits.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
